tldr: Headlines across all nine outlet clusters lead with the lethality of the ICE enforcement action — 'fatally shoots,' 'kills,' or 'guns down' — rather than DHS's self-defense characterisation, which is absent from every title. Three headlines explicitly demand investigation (Globe and Mail, The Hill, NDTV), and The Atlantic's 'Another Fatal ICE Shooting' is the only title to assert a pattern outright; all other pattern context appears below the fold.

Son of Mexican national who was fatally shot by ICE

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only headline to foreground the victim's absence of a criminal record ('No Criminal Conviction') as a title-level claim — a fairness argument embedded in the headline itself while every other outlet relegated that detail to the body or sub-headline.
'Gun down' is the sole use of lethal-aggression register among all English-language headlines; every other outlet uses 'fatally shoots,' 'shoots dead,' or 'kills' — neutral or procedural register.

'Another Fatal ICE Shooting' is the only headline to assert systemic pattern via 'Another' in the title itself; all other pattern context ('at least six/eight killings since January 2025') appears in ledes or body text.
Only headline to centre the experience of secondary notification — a family member learning of a death via social media — rather than the enforcement event or political response.

Sole headline to lead with congressional reaction ('Lawmakers demand answers') rather than the shooting itself, making the US title structurally a political story rather than a crime or accountability story.

bottom_line: The headline layer presents this uniformly as a lethal-force story — ICE shoots, kills, or guns down a Mexican man — while the DHS self-defense framing, victim's personal history, and enforcement-pattern context are deferred below the fold in every outlet except The Atlantic (pattern) and NDTV (victim's innocence).
